subject: english
title: PSLE English — Synthesis & Transformation Tricks (10 marks)

stats.headline: Synthesis = 10 marks. PSLE rotates the same 6 patterns every year. Learn the keyword-to-pattern map and the most common traps — full marks become straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Synthesis & Transformation (10 marks)
keyConcepts.body:
**This is a big topic — but we have identified all the patterns and common mistakes.**

Synthesis asks you to REWRITE a sentence using a given keyword, keeping the meaning. 6 patterns cover almost every PSLE question.
bullets: **A: Linking Words** — Although / Because / If / In order to
**B: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift
**C: Pair Words & Preference** — Both/Either/Neither + Would rather
**D: Participle Clauses** — Having + V-ed, Seeing, Upon arriving
**E: Substitution & Inversion** — Instead of / Except / Little did
**F: Noun Patterns** — Relative (who/whom/which), possessive, verb→noun
**3 rules of synthesis:** ① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.
Pattern **A: Linking Words** — Although / Because / If / In order to.
Pattern **B: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift.
Pattern **C: Pair Words & Preference** — Both/Either/Neither + Would rather.
Pattern **D: Participle Clauses** — Having + V-ed, Seeing, Upon arriving.
Pattern **E: Substitution & Inversion** — Instead of / Except / Little did.
Pattern **F: Noun Patterns** — Relative (who/whom/which), possessive, verb→noun.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Linking Words (Although / Because / If / In order to)
keyConcepts.body: Linking words join two sentences into one. Each linker takes a CLAUSE (subject + verb) or a NOUN PHRASE — never both. Mixing them up is the PSLE trap.
bullets:
**Contrast** — Although / Even though + **CLAUSE**  vs  Despite / In spite of + **NOUN PHRASE**
✓ **Although** he was tired, he kept working.
✓ **Despite** his tiredness, he kept working.
✗ Despite he was tired, he kept working.

**Cause** — Because / Since / As + **CLAUSE**  vs  Due to / Owing to + **NOUN PHRASE**
✓ **Because** the bus broke down, we were late.
✓ **Due to** the bus breakdown, we were late.

**Condition** — If + present, will + verb. **Unless = if not** — flip the negative!
✓ **Unless** you hurry, you will miss the bus.
✗ Unless you **don't** hurry…  (double negative)

**Purpose** — In order to / So as to + verb  /  So that + clause
✓ **In order to** pass the test, Daniel trained daily.

**Conversion trick** (clause → noun phrase): verb → noun, drop subject.
"It was raining" → "**the rain**" · "She was sick" → "**her illness**"

**Common mistakes**
✗ Keeping "not" after **Unless** → double negative.
✗ Changing a content word ("homework" when source said "work") = 0 marks.
✗ Dropping a modifier — "fell ill **suddenly**" → "**sudden** illness", not just "illness".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
keyConcepts.body: Convert direct speech (with quotes) into indirect (no quotes). Headline rule: BACKSHIFT — when the reporting verb is past, every tense moves one step further back. Biggest trap: past → past perfect.
bullets:
**Tense backshift** (reporting verb is past)
– present → past   ·   present continuous → past continuous
– **past → past perfect (had + V-ed)**  ⚠️ the past-past trap
– present perfect → past perfect
– will → would   ·   can → could   ·   may → might

**⚠️ The past-past trap** (most-missed)
Direct: "I **broke** the window," John said.
✗ John said that he **broke** the window.
✓ John said that he **had broken** the window.

**Pronoun & time/place shifts**
– I → he/she   ·   my → his/her   ·   we → they
– today → that day   ·   **yesterday → the day before**
– tomorrow → the next day   ·   here → there   ·   this → that

**Questions & Commands**
Questions: use `asked if / whether`; drop "do/did"; statement order; no question mark.
✓ "Are you coming?" → She asked **if** I **was** coming.
Commands: use `told [person] to + verb`.
✓ "Sit down!" → The teacher **told** us **to sit down**.

**Common mistakes**
✗ Forgetting the verb backshift — "saw" must become "had seen".
✗ Vague pronouns when the original named a specific person.
✗ Synonym swaps for time words — use the EXACT shift words.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Linking Words & Reported Speech

interactiveQuiz.label: Linking Words — Although → Despite
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Although the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Rewrite using "Despite". Which is correct?
options: Despite the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Despite the bad weather, the football match continued.
Despite the football match, the weather was bad.
Despite of the bad weather, the football match continued.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Despite takes a NOUN PHRASE. "The weather was bad" (clause) becomes "the bad weather". "Despite of" is always wrong.

interactiveQuiz.label: Unless negation trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: If you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Rewrite starting with "Unless". Which is correct?
options: Unless you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will not get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will be wet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Unless" already means "if not", so DROP the "don't". "If you don't wear" → "Unless you wear". The main clause stays the same.

interactiveQuiz.label: Past-past trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Direct: "I lost my wallet yesterday," Tom said.
Convert to reported speech. Which is correct?
options: Tom said that he lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet the day before.
Tom said that he has lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet yesterday.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two backshifts together: "lost" → "had lost", AND "yesterday" → "the day before". Options 3 and 4 catch only one.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Pair Words & Preference
keyConcepts.body: Pair words link two equal halves. Preference compares two activities. The trap is the partner word (and/or/nor/than) and the inversion after "Not only".
bullets:
**Pair Words** — both halves must match in kind.
✓ **Both** Ali **and** Lily like to hike.
✓ **Either** play soccer **or** read.   ·   ✓ **Neither** Ali **nor** Lily likes swimming.
✓ Sheila enjoys baking **as well as** her sisters.

**Not only — INVERSION**  (memorise this formula)
**Not only + DOES / DID / HAS + Subject + BASE VERB, but…also**
✓ **Not only does** Sarah **sing** beautifully, **but she also** plays the piano well.
✗ Not only Sarah sings beautifully…  (no inversion = wrong)

**X or Y** (warning + consequence)
✓ Hand in your work on time, **or** your marks will be deducted.

**Preference — three forms**
**Would rather + BARE verb + than + BARE verb**  (no "to", no -ing)
✓ Alice would rather **read** than **watch TV**.
**Prefer + V-ing to V-ing**
✓ Grandparents prefer **visiting** local attractions **to travelling** overseas.
**Have a preference for X over Y**
✓ A **preference for** local attractions **over** overseas.

**Common mistakes**
✗ Forgetting inversion after "Not only" — costs all the marks.
✗ "would rather to" or "would rather V-ing" — bare verbs only.
✗ "preference to" — wrong preposition. Use "for…over".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Participle Clauses (Having / -ing / Upon)
keyConcepts.body: Participle clauses compress two sentences into one by replacing the first with a participle. The new subject moves into the second clause.
bullets:
**Having + past participle**  (action 1 finished, then action 2)
✓ Mary finished her homework. She went out.
→ **Having finished** her homework, **Mary** went out.
Negative: **Not having baked** cookies before, Amy burnt all of them.

**Bare -ing participle** (Seeing / Hearing / Feeling — sensing or concurrent action)
✓ The boy heard a loud noise. He looked up.
→ **Hearing** a loud noise, the boy looked up.

**Upon / On / After / Before + V-ing**  (time linkers)
✓ **Upon arriving** at the station, we boarded the train.
✓ **After finishing** dinner, John washed the dishes.

**Being + adjective / past participle**  (state or passive)
✓ **Being too shy**, Hamid kept quiet.

**Two checks** before submitting:
① Correct past participle (drew → **drawn**, eat → **eaten**, write → **written**).
② Possessives (**his / her / their**) must stay unchanged.

**Common mistakes**
✗ "Having **drew**" — should be "Having **drawn**".
✗ Changing "his / her" to "a" / "the" — meaning shift = 0 marks.
✗ Repeating the subject in both clauses.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pair Words — Not only
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Sarah sings beautifully. She plays the piano well.
Rewrite starting with "Not only". Which is correct?
options: Not only Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Sarah not only sings beautifully and she plays the piano well.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Not only" forces inversion: "does + Sarah + BASE verb". After "does", the verb is in base form (sing), not "sings".

interactiveQuiz.label: Preference — would rather
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Rani enjoys cycling. She does not enjoy swimming.
Rewrite using "would rather". Which is correct?
options: Rani would rather cycle than to swim.
Rani would rather cycling than swimming.
Rani would rather cycle than swim.
Rani prefers cycle than swim.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Would rather" takes BARE verbs (no "to", no -ing). Both halves use the same form: cycle / swim.

interactiveQuiz.label: Participle — Having
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: John drew a picture of his dog. Then he framed it up.
Rewrite starting with "Having". Which is correct?
options: Having drew a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of a dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
John drew a picture of his dog, having framed it up.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Past participle of "draw" is "drawn" (not "drew"). Keep "his" — changing to "a" is a meaning shift.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern E — Substitution & Inversion
keyConcepts.body: Substitution swaps one action for another or removes one item from a group. Inversion flips subject-verb order after a negative starter (same flip you saw with "Not only").
bullets:
**Instead of + V-ing** (or noun) — replaces one action with another.
✓ Ahmad did not do his homework. He watched TV.
→ **Instead of** doing his homework, Ahmad watched TV.

**Except + noun** — removes one item from a group.
✓ All the children were pleased **except** Linda.

**Much to + possessive + noun** — describes a reaction.
✓ **Much to** Gavin's delight, he received the present.
✓ **Much to** the team's disappointment, the match was called off.

**Negative Inversion** — same flip as "Not only".
**Little did + Subject + BASE verb**
✓ Ben never knew he would top the class. → **Little did Ben know** he would top the class.
**No other + noun + verb**
✓ Rita is the only one who can perform magic tricks. → **No other** student can perform magic tricks like Rita.
**No sooner had + S + V-ed + than…**
✓ The bell rang and the students left. → **No sooner had** the bell rung **than** the students left.

**Common mistakes**
✗ "Little did he **knew**" — should be base verb "**know**".
✗ "Instead of he watched TV" — must be **-ing** form: "Instead of **watching** TV".
✗ "Gavin delight" — needs the 's: "Gavin's delight".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern F — Noun Patterns (Relative / Possessive / Verb→Noun)
keyConcepts.body: Three ways to reshape information about a noun. Relative clauses describe a noun, possessives compress a sentence into an 's noun phrase, and verb→noun conversions turn an action into a noun.
bullets:
**Relative pronouns** — pick by what they replace:
– **who** = subject (people) — replaces he/she/they
– **whom** = object (people) — replaces him/her/them
– **which** = things   ·   **whose** = possessive
✓ The boy **whose** dog was lost is my classmate.

**POSITION RULE — the pronoun must sit RIGHT AFTER the noun it describes.**
✓ **Mr Kwan, whom we admire,** is our football player.
✗ Mr Kwan is our football player, whom we admire.
**Comma rule:** defining clause = NO commas (essential info); non-defining = USE commas both sides (extra info).

**Possessive 's transformation** — compress "X was [adj]" into an 's noun phrase.
✓ Sally was insincere. They were annoyed.
→ **Sally's** insincerity annoyed them.
✓ Without **Mr Ahmad's** advice, we would have failed.

**Verb → noun conversion** — change action into a noun phrase.
Common openers: **made + noun**, **came to + noun**, **took + noun**, **had a + noun**.
✓ Vasanthi **lost** her phone → Vasanthi reported **the loss** of her phone.
✓ The diver **adjusted his goggles slightly** → The diver **made slight adjustments** to his goggles.
✓ The meeting **concluded** → The meeting **came to a conclusion**.

**Common mistakes**
✗ Relative clause far from the noun — position matters.
✗ "**whom is**" — always wrong (use "who" for subject).
✗ Wrong noun form ("concluding" not "conclusion"). Must be a real word, spelt correctly.
✗ Dropping the modifier when converting verb → noun ("**slight** adjustments", not "adjustments").

interactiveQuiz.label: Substitution — Instead of
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Ahmad did not complete his homework. He watched TV.
Rewrite using "Instead of". Which is correct?
options: Instead of he watched TV, Ahmad did not complete his homework.
Instead of completing his homework, Ahmad watched TV.
Instead of his homework, Ahmad completed TV.
Instead of complete his homework, Ahmad watched TV.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Instead of" takes the -ing form (or a noun), not a bare verb or a clause.

interactiveQuiz.label: Inversion — Little did
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Ben never knew that he would top the class.
Rewrite starting with "Little did". Which is correct?
options: Little did Ben knew that he would top the class.
Little Ben did know that he would top the class.
Little did Ben know that he would top the class.
Little did know Ben that he would top the class.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Negative starter → inversion. Little did + Subject + BASE verb. Base form of "knew" is "know".

interactiveQuiz.label: Verb → Noun
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: The meeting concluded only in the evening.
Rewrite starting with "The meeting came". Which is correct?
options: The meeting came to a conclusion only in the evening.
The meeting came concluded only in the evening.
The meeting came conclusion only in the evening.
The meeting came to conclude only in the evening.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "concluded" → "conclusion". The fixed phrase is **came to a conclusion** — needs "to a" before the noun.

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from PSLE 2014-2024 Synthesis & Transformation. Covers all 6 patterns. Trains the reflex of "same tense, same info, same meaning" on every rewrite.
bullets: **24 questions** — **4 from each of the 6 patterns**
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI marks word-for-word — partial credit if you almost got it but missed tense/info
You'll tackle **24 questions** — exactly **4 from each of the 6 patterns**.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved, and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your answers word-for-word — giving partial credit if you almost got it but missed the tense/info.

keyWords.definition: Two contrasting ideas joined. Although + clause / Despite + noun phrase / In spite of + noun phrase. The clause-vs-noun-phrase split is the most common trap.
Means 'if...not'. When converting If → Unless, NEGATE the if-clause — drop or add 'not'. Double negatives are the #1 trap.
Purpose linker. 'In order to + verb' (same subject in both halves) or 'So that + clause' (can have a different subject).

In reported speech with past reporting verb, every tense shifts one step back: present → past, past → past perfect (had + V-ed). Pronouns and time/place words shift too: yesterday → the day before, here → there.
Compares two activities. Followed by BARE verbs: 'would rather X than Y' — no 'to' before Y and no -ing. Different from 'prefer' (uses V-ing) and 'preference' (uses 'for X over Y').
'Not only' at sentence start FORCES inversion: 'Not only DOES Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well'. Forgetting 'does / did / has' costs the mark.
Same flip as 'Not only' — after Little did / No other / No sooner / Hardly / Scarcely: starter + helper (does/did/has/had) + Subject + BASE verb.

Joins two sequential actions where the first finishes before the second. 'Mary finished her homework. She went out' → 'Having finished her homework, Mary went out.' Possessives (his/her/their) stay unchanged.
Substitution. Replaces one action with another. MUST use -ing form (or a noun), never a bare verb or clause.
Describes a reaction. 'Much to Gavin's delight…' The 's possessive is essential.
who = subject (replaces he/she/they). whom = object (replaces him/her/them). Test: if you could substitute he/she → who; him/her → whom.

Change an action verb into a noun phrase: 'lost her phone' → 'the loss of her phone'; 'adjusted slightly' → 'made slight adjustments'; 'concluded' → 'came to a conclusion'. Common openers: made / came to / took / had a.
① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.

subTopics.label: Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
subTopics.description: Direct ↔ indirect. Backshift tenses one step (present → past, past → past perfect). Also shift pronouns and time words (yesterday → the day before). Past-past trap is the headline mistake.

subTopics.label: Linking Words (Although / Because / If / In order to)
subTopics.description: Combines concession (Although/Despite/No matter/However/But/While), cause (Because/Since/As/Due to/Owing to/So), condition (If/Unless/Provided/As long as) and purpose (In order to/So that). All share the clause-vs-noun-phrase conversion trick.

subTopics.label: Pair Words & Preference
subTopics.description: Pair words (Both/Either/Neither/Not only/As well as/Or) plus preference (Would rather/Prefer/Preference for). Headline trap: 'Not only' at sentence start FORCES inversion. 'Would rather' takes bare verbs.

subTopics.label: Participle Clauses (Having / -ing)
subTopics.description: Joins two actions by reducing the first into a participle. Forms: Having + V-ed (sequential), bare V-ing (concurrent — Seeing/Hearing), Upon/After/Before + V-ing (temporal), and Being + adjective (state).

subTopics.label: Substitution & Inversion
subTopics.description: Substitution (Instead of + V-ing, Except + noun, Much to + possessive). Inversion after a negative starter (Little did / No other / No sooner / Hardly): negative + helper + Subject + BASE verb. Same flip as 'Not only'.

subTopics.label: Noun Patterns (Relative / Possessive / Verb→Noun)
subTopics.description: Reshapes information about a noun. Relative clauses (who/whom/which/whose), possessive 's transformations (Sally's insincerity), and verb→noun conversions ('made slight adjustments', 'came to a conclusion'). The position rule and exact-spelling rule cost marks if missed.
